data.epi_content:
At first, it was just a small village.

The beginning was difficult, but after that, I could easily offer many people to the heavens with just a few finger gestures, pushing aside the inner turmoil that told me this was wrong while slightly furrowing my brow.

When I first set out to save Evan with dreams of becoming a great Black Magician, I felt a twinge of sadness each time someone died.

Now I somewhat understand how Evan felt when he casually dissolved people at the Academy.

I used to think there was something revolting about killing people so readily, but from a black magician's perspective, it's as natural as breathing.

Human lives no longer look like human lives.

They're just walking sacrifices.

Perhaps I should simplify my thinking.

Just as we didn't consider Reds to be human, these people who wish for Evan's death are evil—not human.

They're just disgusting, talking vermin.

That's why I never spoke to them.

If I conversed with them, I might realize they were human.

I didn't try to learn about my surroundings.

If I observed how they lived, what I was doing would become cruel and unnecessary.

If the thought arose that Evan might not be more important than all those countless dying people, I might collapse right where I stood.

So I forget.

I don't call killing people "murder."

I think of it as hunting or punishment.

It's simple when you think about it.

These things have always existed throughout human history.

Eliminating the families of traitors isn't murder.

If they share blood with traitors, they aren't human because they carry rebellious blood.

Witch hunts aren't murder.

For devout people, it's obvious.

Witches aren't human—they're evil beings who must die.

Execution through people's trials isn't murder.

Reactionaries aren't human; they're leeches who sucked the blood of the people and must be eradicated.

Reds aren't people.

They're too stupid to be considered human, and they're pests who would kill humans if left alone.

After I finished this rationalization and had personally disposed of hundreds, even thousands of people with my own hands...

Several skeletons began following me.

These skeletons killed village guards themselves, stole their spears and armor, and now protect me.

After visiting a few more villages, I earned a nickname.

The Black Magician's Whore, they call me.

Well, it's not entirely wrong since I've been asking about Evan and gathering information about his situation wherever I go.

Looking at marriage broadly, it could be degraded to a prostitution contract promising to spread your legs for just one man.

It's quite an attractive proposition for humans who'd rather raise their own children than someone else's, though it comes with constraints.

Am I being too negative?

Anyway, that's not really important.

Lately, more than a hundred skeletons follow behind me.

I've encountered some knights in armor—one seemed flustered upon seeing me, probably one of Evan's family knights—but I can now easily dispose of knights by lifting them with telekinesis and dropping them in water, though I could barely lift a chocolate bar before.

They probably died.

How could anyone swim or float wearing such heavy armor?
data.epi_content2:
Not my problem, though.

What I need to worry about now is the bizarre being pointing a sword at me, causing cognitive dissonance.

And it happened right in the middle of my hunt.

I had just released a woman whose energy I'd been draining, threw her to the ground, and stepped on her neck.

When I didn't hear a crack, I stomped several times until I heard bones twisting, and the woman lifted her toes slightly before dying.

In her trembling right hand was a bag full of sweet potatoes.

Since I hadn't eaten properly lately, I took just one from the bag and put it in my pocket.

I'll leave the rest for her journey to the afterlife.

I don't know where the afterlife is, but she might get hungry on the way.

The black thing frowns as it watches.

Judging by the blood on that long sword, it must have killed more than a few people itself.

Of course, it probably only killed criminals.

Am I a criminal? I don't know.

Anyway, it's incomprehensible that this madman who mixed with Blacks would teach a Black how to use a sword and not even put a leash on it.

I've seen it before, but it still makes no sense.

I know there are lunatics who even mount goats or horses, but to cherish the offspring that results?

"Are you Erica?"

Good heavens, it's even speaking informally to me.

I realized it hadn't been taught basic etiquette before, but I really don't want to hear this.

I'm not a slave—why should I be addressed informally by a Black?

I've heard it before, but each time I experience it, something feels fundamentally wrong.

It's like watching a civilization ruled by dolphins on land.

"Livestock should wear a leash and return to the farm to pick cotton, not ask people's names."

"Just as crazy as I heard."

"If you speak to your master that way, you shouldn't just lose a wrist—your entire family should be stoned to death, haha."

Something about that triggered a trauma, and the Black's expression contorted.

I just enjoy the sight without saying another word.

Several skeletons, unable to wait, begin charging toward the Black with spears raised.

But there's no chance of victory.

Ordinary humans can be dispatched with a simple thrust, but how could mere skeletons defeat a beast moving so quickly with plate armor and a long sword?

So I pretend to be relaxed, sitting on a skeleton while casting spells furiously.

All while preparing to flee if necessary.

"Tell your master, Vivian, that Evan and I need to talk.

If she doesn't listen, I'll keep wandering around and hiding, killing innocent people everywhere."

"You think she'd listen to that?"

I nod at those words, mocking the Black.

"Yes, you have to stay in one place, but we don't need to."

It seems he didn't come on horseback.

I should be able to escape easily.

"Do you think we can't find you cursed heretics and evil ones?"

"A Black speaking in such an old-fashioned way really causes cognitive dissonance."
data.epi_content3:
I get up limping and whistle.

A dog resembling a Golden Retriever that I once saved from being bullied by village children comes running, panting.

Pitifully, it's missing its left eye from being used as a toy, but its legs are perfectly fine, so it runs very well.

Even with me on its back.

A much more useful friend than a pet rock.

The dog barks and approaches me, but instead of letting me mount, it enthusiastically licks my face.

I gently push the dog away and climb onto its back with some effort.

When I slap its hindquarters, it makes a "kyaeng" sound and starts running somewhere.

The skeletons will follow on their own.

It doesn't matter if they don't.

From behind, I hear nonsense like "stop there" or "are you running away"... sorry to the dog for the comparison.

Anyway, I hear such foolish words, but how could I possibly win?

Spears don't penetrate him, and he seems like he could block a sword with his bare forearm.

Someone like me would be cut in half instantly by that huge sword.

We'll meet quite often from now on.

Whether it's Vivian, that Black, or that so-called Northern Duke or whatever.

Because I'm rushing to the Imperial Palace to meet Evan.

He saved me when my limbs were severed and my eyes crushed, so I must repay that kindness.

If some unnecessary and regrettable sacrifices occur in the process, that's Vivian's fault.

Driving Evan and me to this point, not giving us room to breathe—it's like a cornered rat biting a cat, desperately trying to escape.

"Next time we meet, I want to kick his shin first.

But thinking about it, I only have one foot."

I mutter to myself and laugh with a "heheh."

Maybe I'm lonely without someone to talk to.

A loner.

But I'll meet him soon.

If thousands aren't enough, I'll sacrifice tens of thousands to meet Evan.

Evan must have sacrificed countless things to come meet me.

The world that gave me no chance or anything when my body was torn apart and I died over and over finally gives something back when I harvest and offer others' lives.

Smiling, I munch on the raw sweet potato that the woman I just killed was carrying.

It doesn't taste good since it's uncooked, but I'm probably smiling now.

With an expression similar to Evan's.

I head toward the next village.

Of course, it's not me running but this dog.

Maybe I should name it.

Since there's already an Evan, I'll call it Ivan.

Stupid Ivan.

The way it only whimpers when surrounded by demonic children, being stoned and bullied, yet still wags its tail and approaches people—it's just like Ivan.

"Run, Ivan."

Ivan responds with a "mung" sound and starts running faster.

Smart dog.

Already understanding commands.

Ivan intelligently avoids knights without me having to tell it to, and takes me to villages full of children.

As if, despite being just a dog, it's seeking revenge through me against the humans who tormented it.
